chapters.title: Preface: Educating Literate Patriots
chapters.text:
Educating Literate Patriots

Know then thyself; presume not God to scan; The
proper study of mankind is man.

So wrote the brilliant Enlightenment figure Alexander Pope. To set
the scene for this book, I'll start with Pope plus a personal story:
In an appendix to this book I will offer several remarks about
the unfortunate influence of Hegel on John Dewey-the father of
American child-centered education. To support those criticisms, I
note that I had deeply studied Hegel in graduate school at Yale. I
remember pondering every dense utterance of the Phenomenology of
Spirit (1807). But I could not remember whether the course lasted a
semester or a whole year. So, I wrote Yale to get my transcript, which
I had never seen. It turned out that the graduate course on Hegel's
Phenomenology did last a full academic year.

The transcript also listed my grades and professors' comments
from my graduate courses in the Yale English Department during the
late nineteen fifties. But the most interesting feature of the transcript
was a rubber-stamped notice at the top, which stated in bold letters:
"VETERAN." What was that doing there? What did that have to do
with my grades?

The late '50s was a very different America. Patriotism was
intense in both political parties. World War II was recently over. But
the Korean War followed-during which I had served. That officially
stamped word at the top of the transcript belonged to that era. Many
students who went to graduate school in those days had been in the
armed forces. But some had avoided service. Yale wanted to say to
the reader of the transcript: "Whatever else you may want to know
about the qualifications of this candidate, you should also know that
he served and therefore deserves a hard, respectful look."

It was very moving. In my boyhood we had been schooled to
be patriotic. I still confess to being so. But time marches on. Hegel
and John Dewey have taught us that human history is a march of
progress. Many young Americans have been taught to believe that
patriotism is now passé-"aufgehoben," as Hegel would put it. This
book is partly devoted to showing that Hegel and Dewey are passé-
along with the idea that human history is inherently progressive.

Take for example our national reading scores. They started
showing a big decline in the late 1940s. In prior decades, when
our average literacy was higher, the USA had flourished because the
creators of our political system were skeptical about human nature.
They did not think that following nature was a good idea in our
Common Schools. They were especially wary of following human
nature, which, as they knew from history, can lead to injustice and
bloodshed. History was to be a key study in our schools to alert us
to the ideals as well as to the foibles and villainies of human nature.
Our moral stories were to do the same. In government, the founders
instituted the separation of powers, and they distributed those
powers broadly, to reduce the chance that the worst sides of human
nature would come to dominate.

The "common school" required common attendance by all social
classes. Students would study in common the same science, and
moral tales, and the same cautionary warnings from history about
human nature, its imperfections and its unreliability and villainies.
Schools were to stress science and history and moral tales. History
would make students keenly aware of the snares of religious zealotry
and of human greed for power.

The Founders wanted to create citizens in whom reason and
science and empathetic moral rectitude would dominate over
passion and rhetoric. They wanted to inculcate the principle that
no individual is by birth more deserving and worthy than another.

[page 6]
People could worship as they pleased, but no single religion was to be
established. We the people were collectively to retain ultimate power.
Hence we the people must be well educated factually, historically,
and ethically.

In short, by a lucky accident, both our nation and our common
schools had been founded at the very height of the European
Enlightenment-the great intellectual movement that aimed to
replace dogma with experience and logic. It placed our dominant
educational emphasis on knowledge and reason, equality and ethics.
The aim of the common school was to form literate, ethical patriots.

But a worm entered that Enlightenment educational apple.

Soon after our founding there arose in Europe a reaction to
the Enlightenment faith in reason, with its cautionary checks and
balances. The new ideas proposed that we must no longer rely on
mere reason. Our instincts and our strong emotions could be trusted.
They were natural and holy. The leaders of the new movement spoke
movingly of "the holiness of the heart's affections." Europe and
America were swept over by-romanticism.

The romantics said that emotion and instinct came from
Nature, the agent of God. They also said that human history
is progressive-a confident notion that underlay child-centered
"progressive education." Nature and the "child-centered" theme of
"natural development" were to dominate in education. In Britain,
Wordsworth jingled: "Enough of Science and of Art; / Close up those
barren leaves; / Come forth and bring with you a heart / That
watches and receives." Nature, not history, was to be the new, God-
governed guide in education.

In Germany, Hegel and the friend of his youth, Schelling, wrote
imposing philosophical tomes against the "narrowness" of mere
reason. Never mind the insights of past history. The forward march
of future history was Hegel's concern. He explained that history was
inherently marching forward. So also believed Dewey, father of our
child-centered "progressive" education. He once conceded that Hegel
had left a "permanent deposit" in his mind.

This book is based on recent cognitive science showing that
our Enlightenment Founders were right and that the child-centered
romantics who now dominate our early education have been deeply
wrong. Their mistakes have caused much of the "malaise" that we
currently experience in our hostile, slogan-dominated politics.

Romanticism makes superb poetry, and that is where it
should remain. In educational policy, romanticism has brought us
confusion, disunion, and illiteracy. Our fifteen-year-olds rank 25th
among the nations in combined literacy, math, and science. The
romantics unwittingly also brought us racism by sentimentalizing
and essentializing human differences of race and ethnicity.

Our Core Knowledge school-reform effort has been criticized for
its "Eurocentrism" and lack of inclusiveness. We have responded to
that criticism in two ways. We have included more material from
non-Eurocentric cultures, and we have left room in each grade for
local materials and have even supplied such materials. To see the
extent of such representation the reader is invited to download the
latest version of the Core Knowledge Sequence as well as numerous
separate items, all supplied without compromising the firm,
cumulative buildup of shared knowledge and vocabulary required
for mastery of the next year's topics. This cumulative approach
results in high grades in state reading tests while still honoring
students' home cultures and enabling them to be included.

So popular is this approach among diverse groups that Core
Knowledge schools are hugely oversubscribed by parents of diverse
home ethnicities. (Chapter 1 offers data on that.) Anyone inclined
to repeat that criticism of Core Knowledge as being "Eurocentric"
should look at the current Core Knowledge Sequence, which can be
downloaded gratis from the Core Knowledge website.

Normalized written English has been so durable that our
written language is, like all national print languages, highly stable
and intergenerational. So, educators who continue to object to the
"Eurocentrism" of Core Knowledge should be reminded that child-
centrism is not only a Eurocentric theory; it is also an incorrect


[page 7]
theory. That's why *several* European nations are falling behind, along
with the USA, on the international PISA tests for fifteen-year-olds.
(See *Appendix I*.)

This book says that we should strive to bring back the Founders'
"common school," whose aim was to create literate patriots. The
Founders conceived that Americans ought to be skeptical of human
nature and that our citizens should know enough history and
science to stay loyal to the ideals of equality and resistant to
human pretension and to sustain empathy for human bad luck. The
cognitive science described in this book (some of it very new) will
show that *empathy* lies at the heart of literacy itself. And literacy is
the indispensable agency for sustaining democracy.

We need to re-create an updated version of the Common School
that turns out literate patriots who cast a cold eye on the extremes of
human self-regard and ambition.

[page 8]
### DEDICATION
I dedicate this book to two New Yorkers:
To Jeffrey Litt
and
(In Memoriam) to Albert Shanker.

They represent hope for setting our schools on an egalitarian course.
When the USA takes a wrong direction, derailed from its ideals,
patriots like Al Shanker and Jeff Litt sometimes succeed in getting us
on the right track.

As a brilliant New York City adolescent, Albert Shanker saw
that his mother had to work 70 hours a week. He became active
in the labor movement. As a math teacher in NYC, he engaged in
union politics. As the powerful head of the American Federation of
Teachers, he became the only person in the nation who combined
the intellectual acumen and the political power to move against
the harmful effects that romantic, child-centered education was
exerting upon poor and immigrant children—and on the wider
nation.

Al and I were born in the same year, 1928. We became friends.
After my book Cultural Literacy came out in 1987, followed by the
start of the Core Knowledge Foundation and my writings on the
errors and social inequities of child-centered education, Shanker
picked up the argument and the revolutionary cause. He wrote
pieces in his influential "Where We Stand" column in the New York
Times. He commissioned essays from me for American Educator—the
union journal—and he put me on two committees of the AFT itself.
Then Al died at age 63. And that was the end of his potential
reshaping of our elementary curricula and our chance to turn
away from romantic child-centered progressivism, which lacked a
concrete curriculum. "Where's the curriculum?" Shanker asked.

Al thus called out the moral flaw at the heart of child-centered
education—which is to encourage the child herself to choose (by
nature's guiding hand) her own readings from the classroom library.
To enable that individualized choice of readings, romantic child-
centrists in charge of our education schools created the scientifically
incorrect theory of generalized "reading levels," and they created
unsound reading measures called "Lexiles."

The result has been deep unfairness. Our children's literacy
scores currently tend to reflect the literacy levels of their parents
rather than the abilities of the child. Such social predestination
does not occur in shared knowledge schools such as the "common
schools" that our founders initiated. With some admirable
exceptions, current practices have caused a multi-decade decline in
our reading scores and in the malnourishment of potential talent.
It's whispered that the decline was owing to school desegregation
—thus transforming educational vice into self-praising virtue. But
the decline preceded school desegregation and tragically impeded
the effectiveness of desegregation. For, under the misguided reign
of child-chosen readings, disadvantaged children of all colors have
failed to overcome their home literacy disadvantage.

In the nineteen nineties, Albert Shanker quickly grasped the
news from psycholinguistics and quickly understood the need for
commonly shared background knowledge in spoken and written
speech. He quickly grasped that disadvantaged children were being
denied instruction in taken-for-granted background knowledge.
Hence commonality of reading instruction was required—not
individuality of instruction. With the power of his union behind
him and with the far reach of his weekly column, Shanker wrote the
following in his New York Times weekly essay "Where We Stand":

[page 9]
What we really need—at the very least—are statewide curriculum frameworks and statewide assessment systems. Then, students and teachers in every school will know what kids are responsible for learning and whether they have learned it. (*New York Times* Dec 11, 1994.)

This book will explain why the first state to follow such a principle will jump to the top in literacy and become a change agent for the rest of the nation. Albert Shanker’s death was a nation- weakening, fairness-weakening misfortune. It is for us, the living, to keep discarding the romantic illusions that he combated. This book is for you, Al.

[page 10]
```text
###
And it's for you, too, Jeff Litt, my second dedicatee:

About twenty-five years ago Jeff Litt was the principal of a school in
the poorest section of New York City-the South Bronx. Jeff admired
the Founders' Common School idea as exemplified by schools that
were then using the Core Knowledge Sequence. It offered what
Shanker was demanding and what his students needed-an explicit,
cumulative, common "curriculum framework." Jeff's students were
poverty-struck multi-racial, multi-ethnic children who were being
held back because the child-centered New York public schools were
not following any specific curriculum framework.

Jeff, your first Core Knowledge school was a miraculous success.
After nine years in your K-8 school, all your impoverished,
disadvantaged eighth graders were accepted by top high schools.
From there they went off to college with fellowships. The word
spread about the common school that you had formed. The
philanthropist Carl Icahn bought you six more charter-school
buildings with six more schools with similar results. You put into
practice what Al Shanker was demanding of all our public schools. I
relate the rest of your inspiring story in the first chapter.
Warm gratitude to you, Jeff... and to Al's inspiring memory.
```

[page 11]
```text

chapters.title: Chapter 1: The Ratchet Effect and the Norway Principle
chapters.text:
The Ratchet Effect and the
Norway Principle

Common background knowledge is thus at the core of a
national network of communication between members of
a democratic community. It makes it possible to fathom
complex messages, and to interpret new ideas, situations,
and challenges. Education plays a leading role in passing
on this common background information-the culture
everybody must be familiar with if society is to remain
democratic and its citizens sovereign.
Unanimous Resolution, Norway Parliament (1994)

Human culture has the distinctive characteristic that
it accumulates modifications over time (what we call
the 'ratchet effect'). These unique processes of social
learning and cooperation lead to humans' unique form of
cumulative cultural evolution.
C. Tennie, J. Call, M. Tomasello "Ratcheting Up The Ratchet:
On the Evolution of Cumulative Culture" (2009)

Cumulative cultural evolution takes place when the
inventions in a cultural group are passed on to the young
with such fidelity that they remain stable in the group
until a new and improved invention comes along (the
so-called ratchet effect). Modern humans had a stronger
ratchet than early humans and apes because they had-in
addition to powerful skills of imitation-proclivities both
to teach things to others and also to conform to others
when they themselves were being taught. And so it is, with
this wave of group-mindedness and conformity, that we get
the possibility of cultural groups creating and constantly
improving their own cognitive artifacts-from procedures
for whale hunting to procedures for solving differential
equations.
(M. Tomasello. A Natural History of Human Thinking, 2014)

These insights are a theoretical forward step in conceiving the
education of a tribe or nation. The ratchet effect concerns the
conceptual advancement over time of an entire cultural group. But
it also accurately describes the conceptual advancement over time
*within* the tribe or nation to each new generation. Schooling is the
means of cultural transmission for the tribe as a whole and for
each young initiate into the tribe. The function that evolutionary
psychologists have applied for the whole group-the ratchet effect
-applies also on the small scale to the individual child's movement
from grade to grade as she gradually assimilates the knowledge and
language of the wider group.

In this first chapter, I'll attempt to summarize some key insights
from the field of evolutionary psychology that I have found to
be highly relevant to our recent decline in reading scores and in
our national unity. I hope you, the reader, will also find them as
illuminating as I have found them to be.

Why do the evolutionists call it a ratchet? The metaphor stresses
what they have determined to be the uniquely progressive character
of a human culture-at least for those human cultures that grow and
survive. Our near relatives the bonobos and the chimpanzees do not
gain ever-advancing new group insights and practices over time.
They keep doing the same things over and over across generations.
```

[page 12]
### The Norway Principle in Full
By means of language and a measure of rationality, our enduring
human cultures have tended to improve themselves.
The metaphor of the ratchet stresses that progressive character
more reliably than do Hegel's abstractions, but it's not the kind
of horizontal circular ratchet you find on a screwdriver or socket
wrench. It's the kind of ratchet that mountaineers place on their
ropes and that allows them to climb upward but prevents them from
slipping back down. Note, however, that some ratchets fail to work.
They slip. The ratchet metaphor holds only for successful tribes.
Similarly, not every child successfully achieves the gains needed to
advance from one grade to the next-especially if the ratchet is based
on a slippery romantic educational theory. The non-slip ratchet is a
metaphor confined to successful tribes and successful pupils. Some
civilizations destroy themselves or get destroyed by other tribes
and cultures. Others ratchet ahead.
In practice, the ratchet effect in human history parallels the
success of empiricism in human thought. If it works-keep it-and
figure out why it works. The ratchet effect in human history also
corresponds roughly to the peer review system in science-and with
the study of human experience in history. The American national
experiment has worked in the past because its principles so far have
been consistent with empiricism and peer review-with one notable
exception, our misalliance in education with romanticism. The anti-
romantic Norway Principle enables the ratchet system to work by
enabling effective national communication and unity. Our recent
educational history is poor. Our ratchet has slipped. Our language
scores and our unity have been retreating.
Those two principles the ratchet effect and the Norway
Principle-describe schools that work and nations that move
forward and endure. The educational side of the ratchet effect
requires schools that induce mastery of tribal language and of
shared tribal knowledge. That's emphatically not to be left to
"nature." Leaving it to nature induces the cultural stasis of bonobos
and the chimpanzees, who fail to exhibit the ratchet effect.

### The Norway Principle in Full
Let's look at the Norway Principle of language comprehension in
more detail. It is a statement about the tribal need for substantive
shared knowledge as key to inducing the ratchet effect and creating a
durable and literate nation.
During the fierce debates of the 1980s over my book Cultural
Literacy (1987), which stayed near the top of the best-seller lists
for half a year, there was overseas commentary and reaction to
its argument even as far away as Norway. It induced an ongoing,
positive correspondence with overseas scholars and public officials.
I can't remember who it was in the subsequent years who mailed
me a copy of an article describing the 1994 decision in Norway to
put into practice the linguistic science that was reported in that
1987 book. The article about the Norwegian Parliament was issued
in English by the Norwegian national department of education. The
article was written by Professor Gudmund Hernes and titled "Core
Curriculum for Primary, Secondary and Adult Education," without
stating who had composed the original parliamentary resolution
that he subsequently translated into English.
Perhaps it was Professor Hernes himself. It brilliantly
summarized key linguistic insights and their implications for
democracy. It stated the case so eloquently that not a single
Norwegian MP from any party opposed the resolution. It was
unanimously adopted.
I find its eloquence and insight to be without peer. It stresses
the essential connection between universal high literacy in a nation
and the competence and fairness of any democracy where the people
rule. Could such eloquence also persuade an American governor or
legislator regarding a core curriculum? It accurately describes and
explains the profound connection of shared knowledge and value
with literacy and social fairness:
It is a tenet of popular enlightenment [i.e. the enlightenment
of a whole people] that shared frames of reference must be


[page 13]
the common property of all the people indeed must be
an integral part of general education-to escape avoidable
differences in competence that can result in social
inequality and be abused by undemocratic forces.

Those who do not share the background information taken
for granted in public discourse will often overlook the point
or miss the meaning. Newcomers to a country who are not
immersed in its frames of reference often remain outsiders
because others cannot take for granted what they know and
can do; they are in constant need of extra explanations.

Common background knowledge is thus at the core of a
national network of communication between members of
a democratic community. It makes it possible to fathom
complex messages, and to interpret new ideas, situations,
and challenges. Education plays a leading role in passing
on this common background information-the culture
everybody must be familiar with if society is to remain
democratic and its citizens sovereign.

If Norway's entire parliament can unanimously understand and
undersign these linguistic and social truths, so can our governors
and state legislators. It can remind them of their constitutional
obligations as Albert Shanker formulated them:

What we really need at the very least are statewide
curriculum frameworks and statewide assessment
systems. Then, students and teachers in every school will
know what kids are responsible for learning and whether
they have learned it.

Such equality of shared knowledge was part of the originating
purpose of our early "Common School" started by our early
predecessors including Noah Webster, Benjamin Franklin, Thomas
Jefferson, and William Holmes McGuffey. That word "common" in

"Common School" was meant to include all pupils from all social
classes. Rich and poor were to learn the same things.

Under Shanker's "curriculum framework," individual districts
and schools can use different versions by different publishers and
authors. Hence there would not be a dictatorial propagandistic
"brainwashing" effect of commonly shared information. It would
have the opposite effect. Making voters more literate makes them
more, not less, independent-minded.

###
How Jeff Litt's Schools Exhibit the Norway Principle and the
Ratchet Effect

In that context, let's look at how Jeff Litt's schools have exhibited
both the Norway Principle and the ratchet effect in the low-income
South Bronx. Here is a demographic breakdown of his pupils.

School |Demographics of Jeff's Schools in NYC|
:---|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:
| |#1|#2|#3|#4|#5|#6|#7|
Below Poverty Level|85%|58%|72%|66%|77%|85%|78%|
Black|49%|33%|62%|61%|58%|51%|45%|
Latino|50%|56%|29%|29%|30%|46%|49%|
Disabled|4%|4%|8%|12%|7%|10%|8%|
Homeless|7%|8%|5%|4%|2%|9%|6%|
ELL|7%|4%|2%|2%|2%|8%|7%|
Asian/Pacific|1%|4%|4%|4%|6%|1%|3%|
White|0%|4%|3%|1%|5%|1%|1%|
Native American|1%|1%|1%|1%|1%|0%|0%|

And (thanks to the American Debate League) here is a
photograph of how his pupils performed by the time his
kindergarteners had reached grades seven and eight.

[page 14]
###

These children have just defeated rival debate teams from
wealthier schools and school districts in New York City. Under Jeff's
guidance, these children had experienced for seven or eight years the
kind of schooling that Al Shanker demanded for all children-that is,
a definite curriculum framework and common readings rather than
their own choice of reading matter. Their rivals in the city schools, by
contrast, had classroom libraries with books graded by grade level,
but they lacked a specific, sequenced curriculum framework.
This picture can be interpreted not just as a tribute to Jeff Litt and
Al Shanker but also as the triumph of the Norway Principle and the
ratchet effect over the principle of "child-centered" education that
prevails in the United States.

If South Bronx parents had their way, Jeff's ratcheting principle
would have taken over the entire South Bronx. His schools can
accept only about 200 kindergarteners each year. Nobody leaves,
and by law, his schools must accept siblings of children already in
the schools. So there must be a blind lottery for those 200 or so
kindergarten places. Twenty-five thousand (25,000) kindergartners
apply each year. Word has gotten round in the South Bronx about
what happens to Jeff's K-8 kindergartners. By grade 8 they all
get accepted by select high schools. After high school they get
fellowships to colleges, and as adults they get good-paying jobs.
That's the ratchet effect at work at the level of the individual pupil.

###

In the photo of their debate victory, they also exhibit their
mastery of the Norway Principle. In persuading their audience in
the debate contest, they show that they have mastered the unspoken
knowledge and values of the adult tribe. They know what will please
and impress their judges, and they can bring that shared knowledge
and value to bear in their arguments. That shared background
knowledge consists of both word meanings and knowledge of what
typically can be taken for granted in the audience. In every modern
nation, "literacy" embraces both-not just the vocabulary but also
the unstated shared knowledge and values of readers.
One can therefore make a further inference about the pupils in
the photograph. These children can write well. They gain admission
to top high schools because they know how to speak and write
publicly. Both reading comprehension and writing expertise rely on
familiarity with the background knowledge and values of the wider
society. For that reason, the Norway Principle of shared background
knowledge is a key component of full literacy in every nation.
In sum, the triumphant photograph on page XX represents the
victory of one educational theory over another: the victory of the
Enlightenment "common school" over the Romantic "child-centered
school." And here's another relevant photo behind that result.

[page 15]
This is the transcription of the image:

This photograph above of a classroom library from the 1940s
or 1950s represents the sort of child-centered reading instruction
that has debased our literacy and enabled Jeff's disadvantaged pupils
to win their debates. The ideas behind our current child-centered
"classroom library" suggest why our literacy began to decline.
Romanticism produced "progressive" child-centrism. That in turn
produced the classroom library, which was supported by the theory
of readability "levels," which is a scientifically incorrect theory.

### Why "Readability" and "Lexiles" and "Grade Levels" Are
Invalid.
The above photograph on page XX is thus also the image of
an educational theory. It is supported by a theory of reading
that is factually incorrect-namely the theory of reading levels
and "Lexiles" and "grade levels." Child-centric educators devised
these to enable the child to choose topics of special interest to
that child. Child-centrism is built upon the idea that the child's
natural, instinctive choice of topic will be right for that child. It
will encourage deep interest and foster the most rapid advance in
gaining reading ability. But we know from declining reading scores
that this notion did not improve reading scores. That's clearly not
a forward ratcheting effect. Any hardheaded, practical ratchet user
would conclude that maybe the ratchet is defective. But romanticism
is impervious to empiricism because it is at bottom a religious faith.
It believes in the "natural" God-determined character of the child's
individual instincts and choices.

Forty-four years ago, I reported experiments at the University
of Virginia that showed readability formulas to be useless in
practice. (In the appendix, I have included my 1980 publication on
that research entitled "Culture and Literacy.") Nonetheless, despite
their invalidity, these readability "levels" and classroom libraries
are so central to the child-centric tradition that those elaborate
"measures" of reading levels persist. They have been used as props to
progressivism for so long that we now have accepted these fictions of
general "reading levels" as being facts.

One can quickly understand why they are not facts. In the
laboratory, readability is determined by processing time. But speed
depends on familiarity, slowness on unfamiliarity, and extreme
slowness and puzzlement on ignorance. Readability, word-novelty,
or the length and construction of a sentence are significant
psychologically *only* when the background knowledge needed for
comprehension is already present. *Readability makes a difference
only to people who already possess the background knowledge required
to understand the passage*. Only then do complex syntax and word
"levels" play a role in the actual readability of a text for an actual
reader. In that case, the passage will take a bit longer to process,
but it will still be understood. But when the needed background
knowledge is absent, there's NO difference in readability "levels" for
an actual text, no matter the characteristics of the text. (See the
appendix on "Culture and Literacy" for more detail on this finding
about the inadequacy of "readability" measures.)

So, without somehow controlling for the actual relevant
knowledge of the specific reader, readability is a pointless measure
for deciding a text's suitability for a child. Technical measures
of word frequency and sentence length and sentence form are
not descriptive of the psychological realities of the classroom nor
sufficient for creating a useful and coherent curriculum. The whole
elaborate paraphernalia based on "readability" and "grade level"
should be discarded by any forward-looking state and replaced with
a specific grade-by grade topic sequence as a core that still leaves
room for school choice among diverse materials that offer diverse
treatments of the same topic sequence.

The key measure for actual curriculum-making and text
selection is to gauge the demands upon the relevant background
knowledge of the student. And the only way to take that into account
*is by knowing what the students already know because you have taught
it to all the children in the class*. That's not possible when children


[page 16]
### The Grapholect and the Nation. Another, concept related to the
Norway Principle is the concept of the grapholect. That's the term
that linguists use to describe the print dialect that is used by a nation
in its public written and spoken discourse in print publications,
radio, television, and the internet. The national character of this
standardized grapholect-the national language-distinguishes US
English from British English and Nigerian English and Australian
English. At some point in history British editors and lexicographers
and schoolbook makers did not approve of naming a "pullover" a
"sweater." Perhaps they did not like our Yankee reference to "sweat."

Grapholects tend to differ between nations. The late Chinua
Achebe was once reproached by other African writers for writing
books in English instead of writing in one of the several regional
dialects of Nigeria, now a huge nation of 195 million people.
Achebe's response is available in an informative article, "English and
the African Writer."

His first novel, Things Fall Apart (a phrase from the Irishman W.
B. Yeats) remains an international bestseller. It has sold more than
twenty million copies. In his essay "English and the African Writer"
he observed that the Nigerian English grapholect is now the national
written and spoken language of Nigeria. It needs to be mastered
by everyone for writing, and for reading and listening-and for
national politics and the national economy, and for giving everyone
a chance. He observed that none of the rival local tribal languages
of Nigeria could serve that egalitarian and practical purpose for its
millions of citizens.

And he made another key point: that although the British had
imposed English on Nigeria for their own purposes, the resulting
linguistic universality enabled people to talk to one another and
work together to a degree that had not been possible in the era of
multiple tribal languages. He observed:

In political and economic terms too, this arbitrary creation
called Nigeria holds out wonderful prospects. Yet the fact
remains that Nigeria was created by the British-for their
own ends. Let us give the devil his due: colonialism in Africa
disrupted many things, but it did create big political units
where there were small, scattered ones before.... On the
whole it did bring together many peoples that had hitherto
gone their several ways. And it gave them a language with
which to talk to one another. If it failed to give them a song,
it at least gave them a tongue, for sighing.

One can understand the types of grievance that might
accompany such an initial imposition of a shared language with
its accompanying shared knowledge and value. But the national
military safety and the economic advantages of forming big nations
from smaller units (like our states in the 1780s) have been a fact of
historical desirability carrying benefits that are hard to challenge.

### "Shared Intentionality" and Cradle Guessing: Besides the ratchet
effect and the Norway Principle, there's a third fundamental concept
that that rounds out the character of national literacy.

It is implied in the Norway Principle and distinguishes us from
bonobos and chimpanzees. We alone among the species employ
articulate language. A scientist whose work is featured in this
book-Michael Tomasello-has spent years studying ape-human
characteristics at the Max Planck Institute, and in a series of well-
written books and articles, he has revealed that a unique key to
human language ability is our empathetic ability, starting in infancy,

[page 17]
### Evolutionists vs. Romanticism:
to gauge other persons' meaning intentions from the noises they
make. From this "shared intentionality," as Tomasello names it, we
move to shared word meanings, and we ratchet up from there.
The common background knowledge described in the Norway
Principle is at the core of a national network of empathetic
communications between members of a democratic community.
It is built upon what Michael Tomasello and his colleagues
name "shared intentionality." It lies behind the human ability
to make and learn articulate language. Humans alone exhibit
"shared intentionality" that enables us uniquely to create and learn
articulate language and form tribes that can act as huge organisms.
Our species alone can ratchet up progressively within a culture
because we alone possess to a high degree the pre-linguistic gift
of empathetic intention guessing. That guessing talent makes us
uniquely articulate and social. Accurate language guesses are just
as essential to a cradled infant as they are to a learned professor of
literature or jurisprudence. A grammar instinct may be inborn, or it
may not be. That is still disputed. But the meaning intentions behind
an utterance must always be inferred even when the utterance is
ungrammatical.

Everybody, including infants, must guess unstated meaning-
intentions. I've written a lot on this subject, but in ignorance of
professor Tomasello's work. I hope others will find his insights
into shared intentionality and the evolutionary perspective of the
"ratchet effect" to be as useful to our educational authorities and
state legislators as I have found them to be.
Evolutionists in general have an illuminating take on early
schooling. They tell us that K-12 schools act as cultural ratchets to
perpetuate the shared knowledge of the society. That's the thousand-
year perspective of A Natural History of Human Thinking, by
Tomasello, who says the following in that book about the schooling
of humans. We have:
proclivities both to teach things to others and also to
conform to others when they themselves were being
taught. And so it is, with this wave of group-mindedness
and conformity, that we get the possibility of cultural
groups creating and constantly improving their own
cognitive artifacts.

Evolutionists vs. Romanticism: Tomasello's allusion to "group-
mindedness and conformity" does not fit well with our child-
centered, individualistic, constructivist conception of elementary
education. The child-centered theory encourages children to develop
naturally according to their inborn paths. Jean Piaget stated it this
way:

Children should be able to do their own experimenting and
their own research. Teachers, of course, can guide them by
providing appropriate materials, but the essential thing is
that, in order for a child to understand something, he must
construct it himself, he must re-invent it.

But decades-even centuries-of ratcheted cultural advance and
tribal experience cannot be "constructed" ex nihilo by a young child.
Tomasello and his colleague C. O'Madagain state it this way:

What is inherited is the product of several generations of
innovation, and often goes beyond what any generation
could have produced on its own. The cultural ratchet
can be seen as a kind of collective pooling of knowledge
and cognitive resources in the social group, as individual
innovations are adopted and built on by everyone else.

Our nationwide experiment with child-centered, constructivist
learning has accompanied a sharp decline of our reading scores
and unity. Those two declines go together, because verbal
comprehension depends upon unstated background knowledge


[page 18]
### 

silently shared between author and reader, speaker and listener
across the nation.
Reading comprehension and speech comprehension depend in
each case upon shared, unstated knowledge and on correctly gauged
meaning intentions that alone can specify and amplify meaning.
Our error about the existence of an all-purpose reading level has led
to alarming declines in our American reading scores—and in our
national unity, which, like our reading scores, depends on shared
background knowledge specific to the utterance. When our collective
shared knowledge declined over the past seven decades, so did our
reading scores and our national cohesion.
In the change from communal school readings in shared
anthologies to individual readings from classroom libraries, the
culprit has been child-centered romanticism. Historians of the
romantic movement of the 19th century (like me) can readily
explain why, after our Enlightenment founding, some top thinkers
like Ralph Waldo Emerson and John Dewey, under the influence
of romanticism, urged us to follow nature in our schooling.
Nature, they confidently believed, would benevolently oversee the
child's natural physical and mental "development." Unfortunately,
the gradual effect of this all-conquering romanticism on American
educational thought has been profound.
Under the romantic faith, God and His agent Nature oversee
our Hegelian forward march. Such confident romanticism viewed
human cognitive "development" as analogous to the growth of a
plant. The romantic poet Wordsworth wrote a long autobiographical
poem titled The Prelude or Growth of a Poet's Mind. He meant it when
he said "growth." An early line in his poem goes: "Fair seedtime
had my soul." Our current use of the terms "development" and
"growth" to describe early education are remnants of that confident
romanticism that began taking over our educational thinking in the
20th century. T. E. Hulme once correctly called romanticism "spilt
religion." That's why it's so hard to combat.
Current evolutionary psychologists want you to notice that
they use a different noun than "development." They use the term
"ontogeny." It simply means: "the life-course of an individual."
Evolutionary psychologists stress that the communal shared
knowledge conveyed over generations by education has been
invented by us and is not seeded in our nature. Evolutionists use the
noun "culture," not "nature." Indeed, they stress that our cultural
artifacts and practices may be hostile to nature. One evolutionist,
Joseph Henrich, described our activities this way:

The disappearance of many megafaunal species eerily
coincides with the arrival of humans on different
continents and large islands. For example, before we
showed up in Australia around 60,000 years ago, the
continent was home to a menagerie of large animals,
including two-ton wombats, immense meat-eating lizards,
and leopard-sized marsupial lions. These, along with 55
other megafaunal species, went extinct in the wake of
our arrival, resulting in the loss of 88% of Australia's big
vertebrates.

Such bloody observations are antidotes to the romantic
assumption that God benignly oversees the educational
"development" of the child. Evolutionists stress the diversity of
children worldwide. They conceive of human education as a
ratcheted cultural process. They regard the shared knowledge
and shared print language of schooling as human-made ratchets
designed to preserve social gains and to devise new improvements of
them.
In holding that view, current evolutionists are in tune with
educational leaders of our early republic like Benjamin Franklin and
Noah Webster, who followed the lead of our founding philosopher,
the Englishman John Locke. Locke's political theories guided
Jefferson in writing our Declaration of Independence, and Locke's
psychological and educational theory proposed that the mind of a


[page 19]
```text
human baby does not start out as a seedling poised to "develop" but
as a "blank slate" ready to be written upon.
That's almost right says Elizabeth Spelke in her authoritative
new work What Babies Know. She shows that the baby's inborn
"core knowledge" is highly vague and abstract-a characteristic that
enables a society's caregivers to write upon the child's semi-blank
"slate" whatever shared knowledge the society's elders have devised.
Current science, then, is more Lockean than Hegelian. Human
culture and education are human-made historical accommodations
to local exigencies, rather than being an inherent, natural unfolding.
(Tomasello for instance stops using the noun "development" after
age nine.) Local conditions are contingent and varied. Our social
inventiveness has enabled human schools to foster varied cultures
and has enabled the survival of humans across the globe in diverse
environments-not through natural development but through
highly varied cultural accommodations.

### The Validity of the Cultural Ratchet, Shared-Knowledge
Concept
Evidence for the mainly cultural, non-developmental, shared-
knowledge character of effective human schooling has been
growing. Here, briefly, are three recent examinations of the shared-
knowledge approach in the USA: 1) The consequences of a statewide
policy change in the state of Tennessee, 2) a well-controlled
experiment with 3,000 pupils by James Kim of Harvard, and 3) a
well-controlled longitudinal Colorado study with 2,300 pupils by
David Grissmer of the University of Virginia.
Tennessee: That state recently overcame the baleful educational
effects of COVID by means of shared-knowledge ratcheting. It
was an unforeseen natural experiment. The new state reading
standards had called for a shared knowledge approach to reading
skill. That new approach was well underway when COVID struck.
The accidental timing made Tennessee the only state that improved
its reading scores under COVID. That's a very suggestive natural
experiment. The following comment was offered by the LIFT
publication-acronym for "Leading Innovation for Tennessee"-put
out by Tennessee educational leaders and reformers:
The Tennessee Department of Education shared in their
July 2022 press release: "State ELA proficiency rates now
exceed pre pandemic levels. Since the pandemic, 72.6% of
districts now meet or exceeded pre-pandemic performance
in ELA." This is an incredible accomplishment given
the challenges of school years affected by the Covid-19
pandemic. "... LIFT report, Fall 2022 page 14.
Second example: The Kim study: A big well-controlled
experiment was recently conducted by Professor James Kim of
Harvard. He oversaw a longitudinal study of some 3,000 first
graders. He and his colleagues determined that shared background
knowledge significantly raised reading and learning abilities in
second grade compared with a control group that followed our
current use of child-chosen reading topics. Kim found that there
was a strong difference in reading and learning ability between the
two groups. The shared-knowledge group were able to use their past
knowledge to master further lessons about related topics in science
and social studies. The shared knowledge approach did a better
job than the Individualized-reading approach in securing further
knowledge.
Third example: the Grissmer study. Here is a description of a
seven-year longitudinal study by David Grissmer, as reported by
Robert Pondiscio for the Fordham Foundation. It is described by
Fordham as the number-one education story of 2023. Its results
were achieved by the year-to-year ratcheting effect of shared
background knowledge as in the Kim study. Here is Pondiscio's
summary:
The six-year randomized control trial followed over 2,300
students who applied for kindergarten to one of nine
```

[page 20]
oversubscribed Core Knowledge schools in the Denver
area. Nearly 700 students who won seats in the lottery
were compared to students who applied but ended up
matriculating elsewhere. Researchers looked at state test
results from third through sixth grade. The cumulative
long-term gain from kindergarten to sixth grade for
the shared-knowledge students was approximately 16
percentile points. Grissmer and his co-authors put this into
sharp relief by noting that if we could collectively raise
the reading scores of America's fourth graders by the same
amount as the Core Knowledge students in the study, the
U.S. would rank among the top five countries on earth in
reading achievement. At the one low-income school in the
study, the gains were large enough to eliminate altogether
the achievement gap associated with income. Eliminate it!

Ratcheting at work!

### Conclusion: Let's Start Ratcheting
Besides the general nationwide ratcheting function of the human
school in forming communicative citizens, the ratcheting principle
is also needed to form communicative pupils from grade to grade-
as the Kim and Grissmer studies showed. The old-fashioned shared-
knowledge classroom is gradually coming to be understood as the
latest thing. Advocates of its foe-child-centered schooling-have
complained that such a shared knowledge curriculum is socially and
politically "conservative."

But language conservatism can be quite hostile to political
conservatism. In fact, it is an essential agency of democratic
revolution. Consider the language conservatism of our Declaration
of Independence. It is intellectually and socially progressive. It places
everybody on the same verbal and social footing, as the Norway
Principle makes clear. It fosters a more competent and egalitarian
society. Hence, the shared background knowledge needed to read
general books in the library and to communicate with fellow citizens
promotes equality, unity, and progress in a modern society.

That does not happen naturally. It happens through effective
schooling. Teaching everybody the same things in early grades in
an interesting and engaging way has proved to be the sole road to
high literacy and equality for all. Child-centered individualism, by
contrast, is a social-justice failure. The ratchet effect combined with
the Norway Principle constitute the sole route to high literacy and
social justice in a nation.

Know.

The origins of cultural cognition. Behavioral and Brain Sciences,

chapters.title: Chapter 2: "I Couldn't Depend on Students to Know Anything."
chapters.text:
"I Couldn't Depend on Students
to Know Anything."
Effects of Child-Centered Romanticism from
Two Teachers and Four States

Here follows an interview with two teachers who have experienced
both the romantic child-centered approach and the common-school,
shared-knowledge approach. Dr. Hudak has taught in public schools
of Ohio and in Arizona, and Ms. Kinter in those of Florida and North
Carolina. Both switched to teaching in shared-knowledge, Core
Knowledge public schools in the latter parts of their teaching careers.
They offer reliable, first-hand accounts of the classroom realities
that they encountered in the United States.

This verbatim interview lets us observe firsthand how poor
children from disadvantaged circumstances are being cheated by
theories about children unfolding naturally when allowed to follow
their own paths. I have changed their references to Core Knowledge
schools to "knowledge-based" schools because this book and the Core
Knowledge Foundation continue to stress that we are promoting a
principle, rather than our specific teaching materials, from which, in
any case, no person employed by or governing the Foundation gains
an extra penny.

Precisely how the ideas of the shared-knowledge approach are put
into effect by such materials should be decided by the schools and
by districts-freely chosen materials but not freely chosen shared
topics. Once a state does its constitutional duty by setting forth a
core topic sequence as Albert Shanker proposed, then it should be up
to districts and schools to select their preferred realizations of that
required topic sequence. That way, no one could credibly make the
facile accusation of government "brainwashing."

To sum up the argument so far: The prior chapter showed that
the theory of child-centered learning is both technically incorrect
and socially unfair. Its individualism has been technically sustained
by the discredited notion of "readability," as well as by the
discredited idea that language ability is a general skill that can be
gained by diverse helter-skelter books at just the right "complexity
levels." On those incorrect notions, the school's choice of reading
topics can supposedly be left up to young pupils themselves! I
have occasionally bolded some of the more astonishing revelations
of Cathy and Michele regarding the unfair effects of those deeply
mistaken ideas.

Most people (I find) are somewhat bored with the technical
aspects of the scientific account of cognitive skills: the specificity
of human expertise. But that basic finding is not complex. And
that insight about skill specificity subverts our current reliance on
general reading-comprehension skills and general critical-thinking
skills. One recommended book on the topic is Peak: Secrets from
New Science of Expertise by Anders Ericsson and Robert Pool. It's
an excellent account of the domain specificity of human skills. It's a
valuable antidote to the harmful pipe dream of general reading skill
and general critical thinking skill that currently debases our reading
skill and our critical thinking.

Thus, Ericsson and Pool:

A crucial fact about expert performance in general: there is
no such thing as developing a general skill. You don't train
your memory; you train your memory for strings of digits
or for collections of words or for people's faces. You don't


[page 23]
train to become an athlete; you train to become a gymnast
or a sprinter or a marathoner.

Similarly, expertise in using American English is not the same as
expertise in using English "English." Modern national languages can
be dialects of a wider language group of tribal or national languages.
The modern nation is a big tribe, and its effectiveness as a tribe
depends upon all its members mastering its dialect so they can
communicate, coordinate, and learn. Teaching and sustaining the
tribal language and lore is therefore a chief duty of the tribe's early
schooling.

In the following interview with Cathy and Michele you will find
concrete reports from two experienced teachers who have seen the
issues firsthand with typical public schools in four states as well
as in Core Knowledge schools, have seen the concrete effects on
children (including their own), and have reached conclusions that
reinforce the scientific findings outlined in the prior chapter.

When their experiences are paired with the recent dramatic
multi-year longitudinal studies of the child-centered versus shared-
knowledge schools, the firsthand classroom observations by these
teachers will be seen as reliable and worth acting upon. At present,
no alternative mode of early education carries as much scientific
and evidentiary weight as the shared-knowledge approach that we
abandoned in the 1940s. Our subsequent enthusiastic adoption of
child-centered education has been a mistake that has weakened the
United States and has exacerbated economic inequalities, reduced
patriotism, and heightened resentment and disaffection among
Americans. Let's hear from the front lines as to why that has been the
result:

### First Topic: Where is the Curriculum?
EDH: Is it currently typical of districts nationwide that teachers are
given only that very general guidance rather than specific topics to
teach?

MICHELE: Yes, yes. I told my own children I was going to teach
in a new school where we knew in advance exactly the things the
children had already learned, and we also knew what we needed to
teach this year, so I said to my kids: "Listen, Mom's going to go to this
knowledge-based school." And my daughter said: "Well, that's a good
thing, Mom. Mom, I think I'm going to come to your school." And I
said, "Why? You don't have to," and she said, "Mom, every year, in
science since I was in kindergarten, I learned about plants. I've had
the same packets on plants since I was in first grade." And she said,
"I'm just tired of plants. So, I think I want to go to your school to learn
something else."

CATHY: The way that I met those so-called standards was left up
to me. I may have a particular textbook that I would begin and then
go through the whole year, or I may not.

EDH: That raises another question. Do you mean that normally
in what you're calling "child-centered classrooms," the teacher will
choose his or her own materials and textbooks to meet these very
general standards?

CATHY: Yes. I think back to when I first went to North Carolina,
and I taught science in fifth grade. I had a set of North Carolina
"standards," but I did not have any specific curriculum. It had no
content. Think like a scientist and so on. Here are your standards,
how you meet them is completely and totally up to you.

EDH: So, you're looking at that issue from both angles, as a
teacher and a mother?

CATHY: Yes. The way that I met those standards was left up to
me. I rummaged around to select my own materials.

EDH: So, the standards were never content standards?

CATHY: No.

EDH: They were what?

CATHY: Reading standards, math standards, writing standards.

EDH: They were skill standards, were they?

CATHY: Yes, yes. Very skills based.

EDH: I see, okay. Now I'm getting the picture.

[page 24]
Note: I was "getting the picture" because cognitive psychology
has informed us that such a "skills" approach is misconceived.
The idea of general skills has been invoked to justify the child-
centered approach, but, as I noted above, recent psychology has
debunked the idea of such general skills.

In deference to the idea of enabling the individual child
to develop naturally and to follow her instinctive choice of
reading topics, the teacher is urged to be "a guide on the side
not a sage on the stage."

Those notions of the child unfolding, "developing" out of her
own nature are a key idea of European Romanticism-rejected
by current science.

Some scientists are cautious about characterizing human
education and "growth" as being a "development"-a word
that literally means "unfolding." They prefer the more neutral
term "ontogeny," which means that one's life course is
influenced by all one's experienced factors, including culture
and accident. "Development" is not an accurate metaphor for
any child's induction into a culture.

The teacher interview continued:

### Centers
MICHELE: Another child-centered idea when I was teaching first
grade was that idea of centers-work centers for students, right?
Students move around from one round table to another. You put
these centers in place so that children could explore. There were
literacy and math centers, so I had this 180-minute block. That's a
lot of minutes for a young child to be exploring. I don't even think
I wrote lesson plans the first three years that I was a child-centered
teacher because I spent all this time creating these independent
work centers with the kids. And so, they moved from one center to
another, and I had a bell and after fifteen minutes I would ding it and
they would move to the next one.

After five minutes they were done, and there was usually mass
chaos ensuing. So, then I would have to create more centers because
I knew that the kids could maintain attention for only five to
seven minutes. So, there I was on the weekends at school, creating
twenty-five centers. I had this graph of a movement system to get
the kids to move through, and then at the end of the day, I would
be exhausted. I tried to teach my standards through their interests.
So, my guided reading centers might be like this: In this center, we
were building words about spiders. In that center, we were reading
a reading passage and answering questions about butterflies. In
another center, we were watching a video. In this center, which was
my guided reading center, I might have leveled texts about spiders.

[Note by EDH: Every aspect of that time-wasting, individual,
arbitrary, non-sequential, unplanned, misguided, incorrect
theory and practice makes one weep. The greatest harm comes
to the neediest and to the wider nation.]

EDH: Cathy, could you say a little bit about centers? What was the
idea of these centers and how did they work?

CATHY: The idea behind the centers was that you were able to
differentiate, to meet students where they were, and to take them as
far as they could go.

EDH: What does a center look like?

CATHY: It could look different in different classrooms. A center,
basically, is a small group of students working on an activity
together. Oftentimes they were student-generated, in that there
would not be a teacher working with them. To meet the needs of
students, you couldn't give them whole-group instruction.

[EDH insertion: There could not be a whole-class discussion,
because the background knowledge of each child was so
unpredictably different. That was because their prior classes
had been on different individualized topics. Hence, there was

[page 25]
CATHY: So, you broke it down into small, manageable groups,
with, hopefully, high-interest activities so students would maintain
engagement throughout them, even though you were not right there
watching them.
EDH: In a way, they were teaching themselves at these centers.
CATHY: Correct, yes.
EDH: Because the teacher couldn't deal with the whole class,
because there were such varied backgrounds among the students?
CATHY: Correct. The teacher was usually at the reading center,
where students were reading "leveled" books. So, you were working
on reading skills and comprehension while the other students
were working on different tasks related to a standard, but again,
hopefully, high interest enough that it was engaging to them, and it
would maintain their attention for fifteen minutes. And then they
would switch.
EDH: How did you prepare? You're preparing for the class to come
in, and so how do you prepare who goes where and, physically, what
do the centers look like?
CATHY: It took a lot of preparation time. I would make games
or find videos. At one point in my classroom, I was lucky that we
had a place where kids could watch a video and then I could create
questions right after the video and they could record their responses
to the questions. There would be a file folder of games.
EDH: So how many centers would there typically be?
CATHY: Most of the time it would depend on how many students
you had in your classroom because I always wanted somebody to
have a partner. The idea of the centers is to generate some student
involvement with each other. So. if I was teaching twenty-five
students, you never wanted more than five. It could be anywhere,
honestly, from four to six centers that I had going in my classroom at
the same time.
EDH: So, a lot of this, the interaction between the students, would
be students teaching themselves. Have I got that right? Since it's not
teacher directed.
CATHY: No, it's not teacher directed. There are times when I
would have a listening station where they would put headphones
on and follow along with a book. Or there would be books about
specific topics. We just didn't know what kind of conversations were
happening over there in the centers. I was hoping for the most part
that they were talking about the text that they were supposed to be
reading or they were pulling some meaning from it. As long as they
did not attract undue attention by being completely off task, that
allowed me to work with my students at my current small group.
EDH: I see.
CATHY: Kind of terrible, huh?

### Incoherence and Unshared Knowledge
CATHY: There was a lot of variation among the classes. We had
five third-grade classrooms. You're now looking at 125 kids coming
from every classroom with hopefully the same "standards-based"
education, but not any kind of specific content. I could have taught
those standards through an exploration of astronomy or of the rain
forest, and my fellow teacher might have taught the same standards
through a completely different subject matter-a novel or an old
textbook or something she found from a secondhand exchange.
EDH: So different classrooms at the same grade level in the same
school are teaching different content?
CATHY: Correct. There was no shared content at all. The
next day, maybe they were interested in Blackbeard. So we were
studying Blackbeard. It was connected through the day but it wasn't
connected day-to-day. [My bolding, EDH]

### Critical Thinking
EDH: You're a guide on the side.

[page 26]
MICHELE: Yes. You know, the idea was if you want kids to think
critically you need to let them solve their own problems, and I guess
my argument to that is, give kids a rich curriculum and let them ask
questions about it, and there's your so-called critical thinking.
EDH: On that point: did you actually spend time on critical
thinking as a process?
MICHELE: Yes, I would have critical thinking packets that I gave
my kids, but I didn't see that it made any difference.
EDH: Let us go into that just a little bit more, if you don't mind,
because I'm interested in how much time was spent on your critical
thinking packets, and what is a critical thinking packet.

[I wanted to discuss that more because the idea of general
critical thinking skills is repudiated by cognitive psychology.
It's one of those concepts (like readability) that was invented to
enable student-centered, individualized, constructed subject
matter.]

MICHELE: Sometimes I had to make it up. A lot of times I would
go to Barnes and Noble, which had a teacher's section, so this was
before the internet was widely used. I would find a little reproducible
book that would say "Critical Thinking." There would be things like
analogies and logic puzzles, not content but things of a puzzle-type
nature. I would buy it, copy it, put it in a packet, and then I would say
to the kids (who were way beyond what I was teaching that day in,
say, mathematics) to use their stapled packets. They would engage in
something completely different from the math I was teaching that
day.

What I found out later is that in a knowledge-based,
instructional school children don't have a lot of extra time to waste
on so-called critical-thinking tasks. Every instructional minute
matters because we have so much content to deliver. In a typical
child-centered school, teachers will put these critical-thinking
packets together for kids because they have more time on their
hands.

At my current Core Knowledge school, we just don't have any
time on our hands for critical-thinking packets, because every
subject carries equal weight. In a knowledge-based school, you are
reading, you are writing, you are talking, in every subject area.

### Child-Centered Civics
I turned the conversation to the subject of civics, and citizenship
and democracy, which seemed remarkably absent under the child-
centered, natural-development point of view. It did not seem focused
on the particular historical culture and society the child was being
inducted into! That would not be natural growth! Instead of "What
do I need to know to be a useful, caring citizen?" it became under
child-centrism: "How do I grow naturally and gradually to become
aware of the wider social world around ME?"
EDH: It was the expanding horizons kind of idea?
CATHY: Yes.
EDH: So, you move from the small group to the neighborhood,
then to the city and then to the country, that sort of thing?
CATHY: Yes, correct.
EDH: Expanding horizons didn't give them a lot of history, did it?
CATHY: No, no, not at all.
MICHELE: The social studies standards are such that they learn
only the same old stuff: about themselves, their home, and their
community.
EDH: Oh, my goodness.
MICHELE: Mind you those so-called child-centered materials
were in the form of worksheets or little projects. Let's make a teepee
or a totem pole to study Native Americans, and they could take their
little packets of papers home. There was little to no content. And I
can tell you as an educator I didn't even know where to go to look for
content. But really it didn't matter, because you teach the big three
and that's really where you spend the bulk of your time.
EDH: By big three you mean reading, writing, and arithmetic?

[page 27]
MICHELE: Yes. Going back to my days in a child-centered school,
we did something called project-based learning in my first years. We
had to create an "authentic" problem for students to solve in their
community. So, my colleagues and I in first grade got together and
we came up with a problem. We pretended the local park was going
to be closed, and we had to generate a letter-writing campaign to
try to keep the park open and provide reasons why the park should
remain open.

And so those poor children really believed that they were closing
one of their local parks, because we had to make it authentic. And
there had to have been some notion of ecology tied to it, I don't
know-maybe people were littering or something. But what can first
graders do? They're learning how to write, and we're going to have
them write a letter. It was just so artificial. They could see through it.
They might not know how to write, but they were smart.

### Child-Centered vs. Shared-Knowledge Schools
MICHELE: My son Ethan attended a Core Knowledge school, and it
was the end of third grade. My husband and I were out to dinner
with him one night and my husband said, "So Ethan. What was
your favorite subject in school this year?" And he said: "Rome."
And he began with the story of Romulus and Remus, and I'm not
exaggerating when I tell you that for one hour we sat and listened
to him recite the rise and fall of the Roman Empire in detail. Ethan
was in the first class of eighth graders that experienced a knowledge-
based school all the way through elementary school. They were our
test group. They are all freshmen in college now, and their awards
and scholarships and accolades and university choices are just
unbelievable.

EDH: We know what the end product is. You've just described it
-well-schooled children: college for all. But how would you describe
the difference in what is actually going on in child-centered and
shared-knowledge classrooms?

MICHELE: Okay. I'll give you a good example. In my daughter's
third-grade child-centered classroom, they were given a basic reader.
Some of the stories are excerpts of classic literature, and other stories
are just stories that a publisher makes up and puts in an anthology.

You come to a Core Knowledge-based classroom, and the
students begin their day with the Vikings. During reading, we are
exploring the Viking myths and having a rich discussion about that.
We go to the domain-based read-alouds, and we're now reading
aloud about the Vikings. Everybody feels they're getting somewhere
from one day to the next. And they are using sophisticated tier-three
words in conversation!

EDH: Would you say the kids are more engaged?

MICHELE: Oh my gosh, they love it. My husband and I used to joke
with our own kids. They'd come home from school, and we would
say at the dinner table, "What did you learn today?" And they would
say, "Nothing." And my husband would then say to them, "Well, then
don't go back." And then once they got to a Core Knowledge school
we'd say, "What did you learn in science today," or "What did you
learn in history and geography?" And they talked and talked about
it. Those are the conversations that the kids themselves are having.
And I will tell you, the parents of our Core Knowledge school will
say to me, "You know, Dr. Hudak, I cannot get over, my son and
my daughter come home, and they are excited about school." When
kids come back after the summer, they cannot wait to come back
to school. There's not this dull "Oh gosh we're here again and we're
filling out packets of worksheets again."

These school districts are looking for silver bullets to end the
achievement gap, but really, it's not that hard. Even our neediest kids
are now freshmen in college. These were kids that came through my
third-grade classroom-struggling students. But by the time they got to
the eighth grade, they weren't struggling anymore.

### Effect on Low-Income Students
CATHY: I'm now an instructional coach for a beginning Core
Knowledge school in a very high-poverty situation. Some of our kids


[page 28]
are homeless; some of them live in a hotel. It's a very sad situation.
This school is now currently in its fifth year, and we are starting
to see remarkable progress. What it's all about is being in those
hallways and hearing the kids saying, "Oh, I remember learning that
in first grade," and their being able to build from there. And watching
them in sixth grade talking about the Mayans that they learned
about in fifth grade. You're seeing a change in school culture. The
children are so excited!
It has been hard because the teachers have to go through a lot
of change in mindset to move from a child-centered curriculum to a
shared-knowledge one. But now, for the first time in its history, this
high-poverty school has moved way up in the rankings.

### Cathy on Child-Centered vs. Shared-Knowledge Schooling
CATHY: I spent fourteen years in what I would consider to be a more
child-centered classroom, in which I was a primary teacher from
preschool to sixth grade. I refer to the child-centered classroom as
pocketed, isolated learning. We'd do one thing, then we would go
to a different thing. The subjects weren't connected. So, we did our
best. We'd have our standards, and we'd follow guidelines set by the
school district.
EDH: Tell me something about the difference between the two
kinds of schools.
CATHY: I wouldn't even know where to start. I will tell you my
first memory of being a parent and completely being overwhelmed
with emotion by what my child was receiving at the shared-
knowledge school.
I had a sixth-grade student who was in a child-centered school,
and I had a first grader who was at a Core Knowledge school. We were
having dinner at the dinner table, and I remember this like it was
yesterday:
As most parents do, we said to the first grader, "So what'd you
learn in school today?" And she said, "Mama, we're talking about
Mesopotamia." Out of my peripheral vision, I could see my sixth
grader at the end of the table with her eyes wide open. My first grader
continues to go on about how they're learning about cuneiform
and how Mesopotamia's considered the cradle of civilization because
every civilization since then etc., etc. She's going on and on. And I
remember Taylor, my sixth grader, putting her hands down on the
table and saying, "Mesapawhat? Mama, I'm in sixth grade and I've
never heard of Mesopotamia." My husband and I made eye contact,
and I just said, "See? See? This is why we made the change we made."
That epitomizes the difference in the education they had. Taylor
was the proverbial oldest child. We read to her every night. She had
all the advantages. The other four came boom, boom, boom, boom. I
was in grad school, so we were busy. We didn't read every night. We
didn't do everything we were supposed to do.
But despite that, those later kids flourished in their knowledge-
based primary and middle schools in a way that absolutely changed
our family dynamics. Instead of going to the beach every summer,
we would go places like Ellis Island or Washington, DC.
Yes. I know that might sound goody-goody, but they wanted to
do it. From a parental perspective, you couldn't give me enough time
to speak about the difference in education it makes having a shared
body of knowledge that builds up over time.
When they're in the younger grades, they grow this small seed
of knowledge, and then as it's continued, it cycles up and you're
adding to it. It becomes that whole association and assimilation
idea that I learned about in undergrad but was never truly able to
help my students gain access to. I knew they had it in their minds
somewhere, but they were never able to find it, because they had lots
of little seeds, but nothing built up to a wider scheme of knowledge
they could access and add to.
I see that that's what a coherent knowledge-based school does.
Students can retrieve and connect things. As teachers, we're not
having to guess about their prior experiences. We can absolutely tap
into those prior experiences, and we can help them access them.

[page 29]
In the past, at a child-centered school, it was like, "Do you
remember that?" "No." But now we can specifically say to a fifth-
grade student, "All right, so do you remember when you were in
second grade, and you learned about President Lincoln's desire to
keep the Union together?" And they all say, "Yes!" So, we as teachers
are empowered to be able to specifically tap into that important
resource that helps students build their knowledge forward.

EDH: It seems to me that if I can interpret what you are saying,
our memories are connected to specific knowledge. To build on
past learnings, and even to communicate with the class, you need
common reference points. If you can depend on the class having
some common background knowledge, then your communications
with all the students are better understood.

CATHY: Absolutely. The way that I would compare the two is this:
child-centered schools tend to be more compartmentalized. We take
our students on day 1 and we go as far as we can with them until
day 180. But everything is kind of disconnected and self-contained,
and it's different from student to student. Math did not connect to
science, which did not connect to history or to language arts.

EDH: I take it in your child-centered days when you received
students from somebody else's class, you couldn't depend on all the
students knowing the same things, could you?

CATHY: No. I couldn't depend on students to know anything.
Here in Charlotte in the child-centered county school, we had five
second-grade classrooms, and I was teaching third and fourth grade.
On day one of third grade, my second graders would come in with
all different kinds of content knowledge. It was "standards-based," so
theoretically they should be coming in with the same standards. But
the way that they got to those standards could have been any subject
matter whatsoever. It's much harder to make significant progress
with those students.

EDH: It seems to me that a teacher can depend on and build on
the children's background knowledge and that students themselves
are making more of a community among themselves because they
share some of the same background knowledge from the previous
grade levels. So, it would make sense that a kind of culture and unity
would develop in the student body itself. Is that what you're telling
me?

CATHY: A third-grade class at one of the Core Knowledge schools
I oversee here in North Carolina is in the middle of studying animal
classification. On a recent visit I stepped back and took myself out
of the conversation. The teachers had been teaching a long time in
child-centered schools, one for fourteen years, the other for twenty-
plus. They were both talking about how this is the first time in
their teaching career that students are so excited about what they're
learning that they're checking books out of the public library. One
student brought a snakeskin in, and another an alligator necklace
that someone gave him. The school itself is becoming a community.
They all have a lot they can share. They have all this knowledge now,
and these kids are learning how to access it outside of school. That's
so exciting.

EDH: Any final thoughts?

CATHY: I always try to tell skeptical parents and teachers this:
"Think about the range of students that you have in your classroom
who all are excited by this content. And because that content is
so engaging, think how much easier it is to use this content to
narrow those gaps and extend students even further. You can do
so much more in terms of writing when you put second graders
in an authentic situation where they're writing a persuasive letter
to President Lincoln from the perspective of an abolitionist. Think
of how you can get your students to understand and be more
empathetic about our immigration situation today when they have
gained knowledge from the immigration history that they learned in
second grade.

For me, I don't know that I would still be doing this if I did not
move to a Core Knowledge classroom-because I was so frustrated.
In 2007, when I was able to move and could teach something besides


[page 30]
reading skills and math skills, I said to myself: "Oh my gosh, this is
why I came into teaching."

### Educating Citizens
EDH: I wanted to ask you one other thing. There's a subtle decline
of patriotism in the country that the Gallup poll keeps recognizing
